text: Postoperative perineal hernia is a rare and difficult to treat complication of abdominal perineal excision. There are few records in literature and its repair remains an open issue, since there isn't a shared standard technique. Surgical repair can be achieved using different approaches. Transabdominal, transperineal, or combined abdominal perineal repair. The defect can be closed by primary suture, mesh placement, or by creating a flap with autologous tissue.

Postoperative perineal hernia is a rare and difficult to treat complication of abdominal perineal excision. There are few records in literature and its repair remains an open issue, since there isn't a shared standard technique. Surgical repair can be achieved using different approaches. Transabdominal, transperineal, or combined abdominal perineal repair. The defect can be closed by primary suture, mesh placement, or by creating a flap with autologous tissue.

We describe a laparoscopic repair of a perineal post-operative hernia using a Proceed mesh. A seventy-five-year-old woman that underwent abdominoperineal resection for a squamous cell carcinoma of the anus about thirty years before, presented with perineal bulging and discomfort. The pelvic MRI showed the uterus protruding through the pelvic floor into the perineal area.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic repair of a postoperative perineal hernia, utilizing a Proceed mesh to reinforce the defect.

A seventy-five-year-old woman that underwent abdominoperineal resection for a squamous cell carcinoma of the anus about thirty years before, presented with perineal bulging and discomfort. The pelvic MRI showed the uterus protruding through the pelvic floor into the perineal area.

The laparoscopic exploration shows few intra-abdominal adhesions. Two 10 mm ports are positioned right paraumbilical and in the right lower abdomen, while a 5 mm trocar is placed on the left mammary line at the umbilical level. The patient is in Trendelenburg position, facilitating the removal of the small bowel and omentum from the pelvis.

During laparoscopic exploration, the surgeon identifies few intra-abdominal adhesions and places two 10mm ports right paraumbilical and in the right lower abdomen, along with a 5mm trocar on the left mammary line at the umbilical level. The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg to facilitate the removal of the small bowel and omentum from the pelvis. A peritoneal flap is created, revealing the peritoneal hernia located between the levator ani muscles. The defect is closed with an absorbable running suture, and a shaped Proceed mesh is inserted through the 10mm trocar. The mesh is positioned above the suture and fixed laterally and posteriorly with absorbable tacks, while anteriorly it is secured to the posterior face of the vagina using glue.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap and identifies the peritoneal hernia located between the levator ani muscles. A laparoscopic primary closure of the defect is performed using an absorbable running suture. A shaped Proceed mesh is inserted through a ten-millimeter trocar and positioned above the suture. The mesh is fixed laterally and posteriorly with absorbable tacks, and anteriorly to the posterior face of the vagina using glue.
The surgeon completes a running suture of the peritoneum using a barbed suture, finalizing the laparoscopic repair procedure.
The surgeon performs a running suture of the peritoneum using a barbed suture, completing the laparoscopic hernia repair.

The patient started soft diet intake and ambulation on postoperative day two and was discharged on postoperative day four. She complained perineal pain during the two weeks following the procedure that was successfully treated with mild pain medications. During the six months follow-up there was no recurrence of the perineal hernia. We believe that this technique is feasible, safe and effective and could be a valid surgical option to repair post-operative perineal hernia.
